items.hook: What we’re reading: writers and readers on the books they enjoyed in June
items.body:
Candice Carty-Williams, Patrick Freyne and Guardian readers discuss the titles they have read over the last month. Join the conversation in the comments

I just finished reading Wimmy Road Boyz by Sufiyaan Salam. I

items.hook: The first game of the 1981 South Africa rugby union tour of New Zealand and the United States is held in Gisborne, New Zealand.
items.body: The 1981 South African rugby tour polarised opinions and inspired widespread protests across New Zealand. One of those things you can't un-know.

items.hook: Bafta tweaks film voting rules, expands longlist in key categories
items.body: The main changes are to outstanding British film and the four performance categories.

items.hook: Syrian civil war: The People's Protection Units (YPG) captured the cities of Serê Kaniyê and Dirbêsiyê, during clashes with pro-government forces in Al-Hasakah.
items.body: The Syrian civil war was an armed conflict that began with the Syrian revolution in March 2011, when popular discontent with the Ba'athist regime ruled by Bashar al-Assad triggered large-scale protests and pro-democracy rallies across Syria, as part of the wider Arab Spring. The world is stranger than it lets on.

items.hook: Nostalgia review – Tarkovsky’s yearning cine-poem is deeply mysterious and profound
items.body:
A scholar has an existential crisis while researching the life of a composer in the Russian director’s Italy-set 1983 masterwork

Andrei Tarkovsky’s 1983 film is a mysterious, imagist cine-poem about alienation and belonging,

items.hook: Battle of Lochmaben Fair: A 500-man raiding party led by Alexander Stewart, Duke of Albany and James Douglas, 9th Earl of Douglas are defeated by Scots forces loyal to Albany's brother James III of Scotland; Douglas is captured.
items.body: The Battle of Lochmaben Fair was an engagement in Lochmaben, Scotland, on 22 July 1484 between Scottish loyalists to James III of Scotland and the rebels Alexander Stewart, Duke of Albany and James Douglas, 9th Earl of Douglas, leading cavalry from England. Worth a quiet trip down the rabbit hole.

items.hook: The Dutch city of Groningen defended by the Spanish and besieged by a Dutch and English army under Maurice of Orange, capitulates.
items.body: Groningen is the capital city and main municipality of Groningen province in the Netherlands. The world is stranger than it lets on.

items.hook: The rise of the ‘hot literati’: how reading became sexy
items.body:
Celebrities are hosting literary clubs and designers are launching products emblazoned with the classics – but does reading’s trendy turn have a darker side?

From

items.hook: Judge orders pause on Paramount-Warner merger after challenge from 12 states
items.body:
$81bn merger halted for at least two weeks after US states sued to block deal, saying it would ‘extinguish competition’

A federal judge on Monday ordered Paramount a

items.opener: Here's one for the group chat —
items.hook: Ethel Mary Wood CBE born Ethel Mary Hogg was an advertising executive, feminist campaigner, philanthropist and collector of bibles.
items.body: She was the daughter of Quintin Hogg, a businessman who established the Regent Street Polytechnic. The world is stranger than it lets on.

items.hook: Matthijs Wouter Knol, Athina Rachel Tsangari among Sarajevo competition jurors
items.body: Croatian director Igor Bezinović and North Macedonian actor Sara Klimoska have also joined the jury.

items.hook: Joseph Battell was an American publisher and philanthropist from Middlebury, Vermont.
items.body: Battell is credited with preserving Vermont forest land including the land for Camel's Hump State Park. One of those things you can't un-know.

items.hook: The first ever motor race is held in France between the cities of Paris and Rouen. The fastest finisher was the Comte Jules-Albert de Dion, but the "official" victory was awarded to Albert Lemaître driving his three-horsepower petrol engined Peugeot.
items.body: Paris–Rouen was a pioneering city-to-city motoring competition in 1894 which is sometimes described as the world's first competitive motor race. One of those things you can't un-know.

items.hook: Gretna is an unincorporated urban community in the Municipality of Rhineland within the Canadian province of Manitoba that held town status prior to January 1, 2015.
items.body: Just north of the Canada - United States border on PTH 30, Gretna had a population of 541 in 2016. Worth a quiet trip down the rabbit hole.

items.hook: Johann Baptist Friedreich was a German forensic physician and psychiatrist.
items.body: He was a prominent member of the so-called "somatic school" of psychiatry in Germany. Bring it up somewhere. Watch the reaction.

items.hook: The Delmé Quartet, aka The Delmé String Quartet, was a String quartet, founded in London in 1962.
items.body: In 1967, it became the first string quartet to be attached to a British university as Artist-in-residence—in this case, the University of Sussex. Bring it up somewhere. Watch the reaction.

items.hook: ‘I come from a murderer’: the woman who found out Charles Manson was her grandfather
items.body:
Difficult new Hulu documentary follows a 26-year-old woman’s startling discovery that she’s related to the notorious, murderous cult leader

Sophia Maddox spent her life hearing from her father about how he had never met his father.

items.hook: Rif War: The Spanish Army suffers its worst military defeat in modern times to the Berbers of the Rif region of Spanish Morocco.
items.body: The Rif War was an armed conflict fought from 1921 to 1926 between Spain and Berber tribes of the mountainous Rif region of northern Morocco. The world is stranger than it lets on.
